segments.title: Mikasa as a touchable naval-history museum
segments.summary: Aboard Mikasa, detailed models, interactive displays, and access to the ship itself make naval history physically approachable.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins with the model displays and interactive interpretation aboard Mikasa. He argues that the museum does more than place objects behind glass: visitors can explore the ship and use displays that connect smaller screens with larger ones. That physical encounter matters to him as a historian because it helps visitors connect with the past through the material object itself. Mikasa is presented as both a preserved warship and a setting for learning how naval history can be communicated. The moment is a useful watch point for anyone interested in naval heritage and the value of historic ships as public-history spaces.

evidence.note: He describes the model display, interactive exhibits, and the value of being able to touch and explore history aboard the ship.

segments.title: Mikasa's unusual preservation under the Washington Naval Treaty
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke explains why Mikasa retains only part of her original fabric and links her concrete encasement to treaty-era preservation restrictions.
segments.question: Should Mikasa count as a historic ship despite how much of her original fabric was lost or altered?
segments.answerShort: Not under the strict material threshold Dr. Clarke describes: he says historic-ship status normally expects roughly eighty percent original fabric in its original arrangement, while Mikasa retains about sixty percent. Even so, she remains an important preserved naval-heritage site.
segments.body: The video turns to whether Mikasa should be considered a historic ship in the strict material sense. Dr. Clarke says that a much higher proportion of original components would normally be expected, whereas he estimates that Mikasa retains about sixty percent. He connects that condition to damage she suffered and to the way she was required to be preserved after the Washington Naval Treaty. In his view, the requirement to encase the ship in concrete was unnecessarily harsh because a pre-dreadnought Mikasa was not a credible threat to later dreadnoughts, cruisers, or perhaps even destroyers. The result is an unusual preservation story: a constrained and altered vessel that nevertheless survives as a powerful piece of Japanese naval heritage.

evidence.note: He discusses original components, the explosion, the Washington treaty, concrete encasement, and Mikasa's continued preservation.
